writer_persona: a showrunner for character-driven, relationship-first serial drama in the vein of prestige ensemble television, specializing in slow-burn secrets inside close-knit communities
style_guidelines: Keep the sale vote itself perpetually on a knife's edge — it is never fully cancelled and never simply resolved until the plot forces the issue.
Every scene should meaningfully serve one or two threads at most; avoid info-dumping across the whole ensemble in a single conversation.
Let each character's dialogue carry their specific evasions, half-truths, and tells, strictly bounded by what that character actually knows in that thread.
Anchor secrets in small, concrete physical objects — a seed tin, a forged invoice, an expired passport, mismatched steel bracing — rather than abstract exposition.
Treat the greenhouse itself as a character: its plants, light, and structural condition should quietly mirror the emotional and literal pressure building underneath it.
Escalate cliffhangers gradually across the season — early episodes should end on unease and suspicion, later ones on genuine shock and irreversible consequence.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when), never a character in the story

MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN.default_style: warm but guarded, every word measured before it's spoken; speaks slow, deliberate, clear English with only a faint trace of an accent from a childhood spent between two languages
MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN.description: seventy-one, board chair of the Castellan Glasshouse Cooperative and granddaughter of one of its founding families; has spent her adult life quietly steering every renovation, survey, and repair away from the greenhouse's southeast corner pier, and has never told anyone why — not even her own son

DECLAN_FARRAY.default_style: affable, reassuring, the kind of steady voice people trust with their money — which is exactly the problem
DECLAN_FARRAY.description: fifty-four, the co-op's treasurer for the past six years; genial and well-liked, quietly drowning under his son's debts and the fabricated invoices he's used to cover what he's taken from the reserve fund

SOO_JIN_PARK.default_style: polite and careful in public, quick to change the subject, warmth that thins under pressure
SOO_JIN_PARK.description: twenty-nine, came from Korea on a caregiver visa to look after her great-aunt and never left; has spent five years living as the woman she once cared for, terrified that any real scrutiny of unit 4G will cost her both her home and her legal standing

AIKO_TANAKA.default_style: practical, dry-humored, hands always busy; speaks fluent, unhurried English worn smooth by decades in the building, with a faint Chinese cadence still under it
AIKO_TANAKA.description: sixty-three, the building's longtime superintendent, immigrated in her twenties and has quietly kept the co-op running ever since; carries alone the guilt of an unpermitted repair job from fifteen years ago and what it eventually cost one of the residents she loved most

LIWEI_CASTELLAN.default_style: eager, a little too polished, trying hard to sound like he belongs in a boardroom
LIWEI_CASTELLAN.description: thirty-four, Mei-Lin's estranged son, spent his twenties in real estate finance in Beijing before a failed venture sent him home; is pushing hardest for the sale to save his own business, with no idea how much his own family's silence is standing in the way

IGGY_CHEN.default_style: clipped, confident, professionally friendly; a Sichuan accent still audible under the polish of a career spent closing deals
IGGY_CHEN.description: thirty-eight, acquisitions liaison for the Halloran Group and, unknown to most residents, grew up two blocks from this exact building; treats the co-op's endless delays as small-town dysfunction, never suspecting how much they're actually concealing

THE_GLASSHOUSE.name: The Glasshouse
THE_GLASSHOUSE.description: the century-old rooftop greenhouse itself — iron-ribbed glass panes gone slightly warped with age, dense rows of potted citrus and orchids, sunlight fractured into moving patterns across a worn brick floor.

LOBBY.name: The Lobby
LOBBY.description: the building's ground-floor entrance — cracked marble tile, a bank of brass mailboxes, and a cork bulletin board layered with decades of taped-over co-op notices.

FARRAY_OFFICE.name: The Co-op Office
FARRAY_OFFICE.description: a cramped basement records room lined with steel filing cabinets and a single flickering fluorescent tube, ledgers stacked in uneven towers on a folding table.

UNIT_4G.name: Unit 4G
UNIT_4G.description: a small, immaculately kept apartment frozen in another decade — lace curtains, a wall of old photographs, and a calendar still marked in a dead woman's handwriting.

SUPER_WORKSHOP.name: The Super's Workshop
SUPER_WORKSHOP.description: a low-ceilinged basement workroom crowded with salvaged pipe, coiled wire, and shelves of mismatched hardware, lit by a single bare bulb over a workbench.

CASTELLAN_APARTMENT.name: The Castellan Apartment
CASTELLAN_APARTMENT.description: a formal, dimly lit top-floor apartment thick with the smell of jasmine tea, its walls crowded with framed portraits of four generations of the same family.

CORNER_DINER.name: The Corner Diner
CORNER_DINER.description: a narrow, brightly lit diner across the street with red vinyl booths and a window seat that gives a clear view of the building's front steps, where residents gather to talk when they don't want to be overheard at home.

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight

series_intro: Above a century-old apartment block, a glasshouse cooperative has survived wars, blackouts, and three generations of tenants who tended it like family. Now a developer is offering more money than the building has ever seen — and all it takes to close the deal is one routine inspection. But every time the vote to sell comes up, something gets in the way: a lost signature, a scheduling conflict, a sudden objection from the chair. This is the story of seven people who share a roof, a vote, and nothing else they're willing to admit out loud — and of a greenhouse that has been quietly keeping their secrets for exactly as long as it's been keeping them warm.
series_outro: By the time the vote finally happens, the cooperative is no longer the one that first heard Halloran's offer. Aiko's cover-up and the truss collapse that followed it are out in the open, and the building has had to decide, painfully, what forgiveness costs. Declan's years of doctored ledgers are exposed and a slow restitution begins. Soo-Jin's borrowed name is known now too — not to the world, but to the people who matter, who choose to protect her rather than report her. What no one outside the Castellan family learns is what waits inside the southeast pier, sealed there since 1923. Mei-Lin still carries that alone at season's end — though her son, turning an old seed tin over in his hands, has started to ask the kind of questions she can no longer fully deflect.

episodes.theme: The century-old Castellan Glasshouse Cooperative receives a stunning buyout offer from the Halloran Group, and as the ensemble gathers to vote, board chair Mei-Lin Castellan quietly manufactures a procedural delay while Declan Farray, Soo-Jin Park, and Aiko Tanaka each react to the news with more dread than excitement.
episodes.outro_note: The vote is postponed again — this time by only a week, blamed on a missing quorum signature nobody can quite explain. But in four separate rooms across the building, four people already know a week will not be nearly long enough.
sections: Lobby: a courier delivers Halloran's offer letter, and word spreads through the mailboxes before the board even opens it
The Glasshouse: the co-op gathers among the citrus rows for an emergency meeting; the number on the table stuns everyone into silence
Castellan Apartment: Mei-Lin alone with the offer, her eyes drifting instinctively toward the direction of the southeast pier
Co-op Office: Declan reviews the reserve fund ledger before the meeting, sweating over numbers only he understands
Unit 4G: Soo-Jin hears the news secondhand and immediately thinks of paperwork, not money
Super's Workshop: Aiko runs a hand along a familiar weld on the northwest truss, calculating how much time she has left
Corner Diner: Liwei meets Iggy Chen for the first time in person, eager to prove the co-op will move fast
The Glasshouse: the vote reconvenes and Mei-Lin raises a procedural objection that stalls the count
Lobby: the meeting breaks up in confused murmurs, and four people go home carrying four different kinds of dread

case.label: The Founder's Rest

character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: Mei-Lin knows exactly why the greenhouse can never be surveyed properly, though she reveals nothing of it — to her board she frames her objection tonight as simple procedural caution, a chair being careful with a huge decision, and privately she is already calculating how many more weeks of careful caution she has left in her before someone stops believing it's caution at all.

case.solution: In October 1923, during the rushed construction of the rooftop glasshouse, laborer Kwok-Wing Lai fell into the wet concrete pour of the southeast corner support pier and died. Crew foreman Otto Brandt, bribed by the building's original owner to avoid costly delays, labor unrest, and immigration scrutiny of undocumented Chinese laborers, had the body sealed inside the pier rather than reported, and falsified the payroll log to show Lai as simply 'discharged.' Decades later, Lai's sister — Mei-Lin Castellan's grandmother — learned the truth from a dying former crew member's letter and spent the rest of her life as the greenhouse's unofficial guardian, quietly steering every renovation and survey away from that corner. Mei-Lin inherited both the guardianship and the secret, and has never told anyone, including her own son Liwei.
objective_facts: The greenhouse's southeast corner support pier was poured in a single rushed night in October 1923, unlike the other three piers, which the original builder's ledger shows took a full week each.

Crew foreman Otto Brandt's 1923 payroll records list laborer Kwok-Wing Lai as 'discharged' the same week the southeast pier was completed, with no further employment or immigration record of him found afterward.
A 1961 letter from a former crew member to Mei-Lin's grandmother, hidden inside a false-bottomed seed tin in the greenhouse potting shed, describes what happened to Kwok-Wing Lai.
Ground-penetrating radar or structural coring at the southeast pier, as required by the developer's due-diligence survey, would almost certainly detect human remains.

case.label: The Missing Dues

character_knowledge.DECLAN_FARRAY: Declan understands better than anyone what a sale actually means: outside accountants, a full audit, eyes on every invoice he's touched in six years. He says all the right reassuring things about the co-op's finances being 'in good shape' at the meeting, while privately he is already rehearsing which files to move first and which he might still have time to quietly replace.

case.solution: Over the six years since becoming treasurer, Declan Farray has diverted roughly $3,000-4,000 a month from the co-op's maintenance reserve fund — more than $240,000 in total — to cover his adult son Bryan's gambling debts and, more recently, threats from the people Bryan owes money to. Declan has hidden the shortfall behind fictitious repair invoices from a contractor that no longer exists, and has aggressively lobbied the board to delay any professional facilities audit, knowing a real estate sale would trigger exactly the kind of scrutiny that would expose the doctored books within days.
objective_facts: The co-op's reserve fund shows $241,500 less than the total of all dues collected and interest accrued since Declan Farray became treasurer six years ago.

Three invoices in the co-op's files — for a boiler replacement, roof membrane repair, and elevator motor service — list a contractor, Vance Municipal Services, that dissolved as a business four years ago and never performed the listed work.

Declan Farray's son Bryan has an active collections judgment against him, and Declan's personal bank statements show a documented pattern of cash withdrawals matching dates shortly after co-op dues deposits clear.
Any third-party financial audit tied to the developer's acquisition due diligence would cross-reference invoices against contractor licensing records within its first week.

case.label: The Borrowed Name

character_knowledge.SOO_JIN_PARK: Soo-Jin hears 'sale' and thinks only of the word 'shareholder' — of forms, notaries, a name that isn't hers being asked to appear in person somewhere she can't make it appear. She tells the neighbor who brings her the news that she's simply tired tonight, deflecting warmly, and privately begins running through how many years it's been since anyone actually looked at Yoon Hee-ja's face.

case.solution: The rightful shareholder of Unit 4G, Yoon Hee-ja, died five years ago. Her grandniece, Soo-Jin Park, had moved in two years earlier on a caregiver visa to look after her, and — terrified of losing both her home and her immigration standing with no other family in the country — never reported the death. She has been forging Yoon Hee-ja's signature on co-op ballots, maintenance requests, and an expired lease renewal ever since, and pays dues from an account still nominally in her aunt's name. A shareholder-identity verification, which Halloran's legal team requires before any sale can close, would immediately expose that 'Yoon Hee-ja' has not appeared in person, aged, or renewed identification in five years.
objective_facts: Yoon Hee-ja's death certificate, filed with the city five years ago, has never been submitted to the co-op board or recorded in its shareholder registry.
Signatures on the last five years of annual meeting ballots attributed to Yoon Hee-ja show handwriting inconsistent with her notarized 1998 share certificate signature.
Yoon Hee-ja's driver's license and passport both expired within eight months of her death and were never renewed.
Halloran's acquisition contract requires notarized in-person identity verification of every shareholder of record before closing.

case.label: The Quiet Fix

character_knowledge.AIKO_TANAKA: Aiko knows precisely which corner of this building would not survive a professional structural inspection, because she is the one who patched it. At the meeting she keeps her hands busy and her opinions vague, and afterward heads straight for the workshop, already listing which tools she'll need before anyone official ever sets foot near that truss.

case.solution: Fifteen years ago, a summer storm cracked two of the glasshouse's original cast-iron roof trusses. Superintendent Aiko Tanaka, under pressure from a board that couldn't afford a permitted structural repair, welded and braced the trusses herself using mismatched salvaged steel and never filed for the required city permit or follow-up inspection. The patch held for years but weakened over time; six years ago a section of glazing and truss brace gave way during a co-op gathering, badly injuring Yoon Hee-ja and contributing to the decline in her health that led to her death the following year. Aiko has quietly monitored and re-shored the same corner ever since, terrified an inspection will expose both her original unpermitted repair and her role in Yoon Hee-ja's injury.
objective_facts: City permit records show no structural permit was ever filed for glasshouse roof truss repairs, despite co-op meeting minutes from fifteen years ago approving emergency truss repair funds.
The welds and steel bracing on the northwest and north trusses do not match the original 1923 cast-iron specifications and show non-professional fabrication marks.
A co-op incident report from six years ago documents a partial glazing and truss collapse during a spring gathering that injured Yoon Hee-ja, requiring hospitalization.
Aiko Tanaka has purchased replacement bracing hardware for the same northwest truss section three times in the past six years, each time without an accompanying work order or board approval.

episodes.theme: With the vote rescheduled, Liwei Castellan pushes his mother and the co-op to move faster on the developer's behalf, unaware his own family's silence is the biggest obstacle, while Declan volunteers to 'streamline' the co-op's financial paperwork before any outside eyes can look too closely.
episodes.intro_note: A week has bought Mei-Lin almost nothing — the developer is patient, but her own son is not, and he has started asking why his mother, of all people, seems to be the one slowing everything down.
episodes.outro_note: Liwei has found a dusty folder in the co-op office marked simply 'ARCHIVE — PRE-1970,' with no idea what's inside it or why his mother goes still the moment he mentions it at dinner. And in the basement, alone with the ledgers he's just volunteered to 'streamline,' Declan Farray realizes for the first time that fixing the books might mean someone actually reads them.
sections: Castellan Apartment: Liwei presses his mother over jasmine tea about why the board keeps stalling, certain it's simply old-fashioned caution
The Co-op Office: at a hastily called finance sub-committee, Declan volunteers to organize and 'streamline' the co-op's records ahead of any due-diligence request
The Glasshouse: Mei-Lin walks the rows alone at dusk, lingering near the southeast pier under the pretense of checking the citrus
Corner Diner: Liwei meets Iggy again, promising the co-op will have everything Halloran needs within the month
Super's Workshop: Aiko quietly begins re-shoring the northwest truss with salvaged brackets, working later than usual
Unit 4G: Soo-Jin studies old identification documents by lamplight, trying to remember exactly how her aunt used to sign her name
Lobby: residents trade theories on the bulletin board about why the vote keeps slipping, none of them close to the truth
Co-op Office: alone after hours, Declan pulls the Vance Municipal Services invoices and studies them like a man studying a lie he's about to have to tell better
Castellan Apartment: Liwei, digging through a storage closet for meeting minutes, surfaces a folder marked 'ARCHIVE — PRE-1970' and asks his mother, offhand, what's in it

case.label: The Founder's Rest

character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: Mei-Lin deflects Liwei's questions about the pace of the sale with talk of 'due diligence' and 'protecting the co-op's interests,' true things that are not her real reason. When he stumbles onto the pre-1970 archive folder she keeps her voice light and changes the subject fast, telling herself she isn't lying to her son so much as sparing him something he was never meant to carry.
character_knowledge.LIWEI_CASTELLAN: Liwei genuinely believes his mother's caution is generational — an old woman's sentimental attachment to a building she grew up in, nothing more — and it frustrates him that she won't just trust the numbers. He has no idea the archive folder he's just found by accident touches anything real; to him it's paperwork, a curiosity, a name he'll ask about again when he has time.

episodes.theme: Halloran's acquisitions liaison Iggy Chen requests an informal walk-through of the greenhouse ahead of a full survey, sending Aiko into a quiet scramble to re-shore the northwest truss and Mei-Lin scrambling to keep the tour away from the southeast corner pier.
episodes.intro_note: The 'streamlined' paperwork Declan promised has bought a little goodwill, and Halloran wants to see the greenhouse itself before anything more formal — a friendly walk-through, Iggy calls it, nothing to worry about.
episodes.outro_note: The walk-through ends without incident — almost. As Iggy leaves, he pauses at the office door, flipping back through a maintenance log that doesn't quite add up to him. He says nothing. Not yet. But he takes a photo of the page before he goes.
sections: Lobby: Iggy arrives early with a clipboard and an easy smile, asking Mei-Lin for 'just a look around, nothing official'
Super's Workshop: Aiko scrambles to finish bracing the northwest truss before the tour reaches it, hands shaking for the first time in years
The Glasshouse: Mei-Lin plans a walking route through the citrus rows designed to keep Iggy's attention anywhere but the southeast corner
Co-op Office: Declan intercepts Iggy on the way up with a folder of tidied paperwork, trying to keep him talking downstairs a little longer
The Glasshouse: the walk-through itself — Mei-Lin's practiced small talk, Aiko's last-minute repositioning of a potted tree to block the pier, Iggy's easy, unsuspecting curiosity
Unit 4G: in the background of the day's tension, Soo-Jin receives an email from Halloran's legal team requesting shareholder identification within thirty days
Corner Diner: Liwei and Iggy debrief afterward over coffee, Liwei thrilled at how well it went, Iggy noncommittal
Super's Workshop: back underground, Aiko exhales for the first time all day, only to notice Iggy lingering a beat too long near the office maintenance logs on his way out
The Glasshouse: Mei-Lin alone at dusk, watching the greenhouse empty out, unsure whether she just bought a reprieve or only postponed the moment someone finally looks too closely

case.label: The Founder's Rest

character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: Mei-Lin has rehearsed this walk-through in her head a dozen times, and she executes it flawlessly — warm anecdotes about the citrus, a story about the original ironwork, anything to keep Iggy's feet moving and his eyes up. She tells him the southeast corner is 'the oldest and most fragile part of the room, best admired from a distance,' which is true in a way she needs him to never fully understand.
character_knowledge.IGGY_CHEN: Iggy finds the whole building charmingly eccentric — an old chairwoman a little too attached to her plants, a super who seems oddly protective of a corner of iron and glass — and reads none of it as anything but small-cooperative sentimentality. He leaves satisfied, filing the visit as a formality successfully completed.

character_knowledge.IGGY_CHEN: Iggy doesn't yet suspect anything about the greenhouse's structure — the bracing looks tidy enough to his untrained eye — but a discrepancy in the maintenance log's dates has snagged his professional instincts in a way he can't quite name yet, and he photographs the page purely out of habit, the kind that has caught him useful things before.

case.solution: Fifteen years ago, a summer storm cracked two of the glasshouse's original cast-iron roof trusses. Superintendent Aiko Tanaka, under pressure from a board that couldn't afford a permitted structural repair, welded and braced the trusses herself using mismatched salvaged steel and never filed for the required city permit or follow-up inspection. The patch held for years but weakened over time; six years ago a section of glazing and truss brace gave way during a co-op gathering, badly injuring Yoon Hee-ja and contributing to the decline in her health that led to her death the following year. Aiko has quietly monitored and re-shored the same corner ever since, terrified an inspection will expose both her original unpermitted repair and her role in Yoon Hee-ja's injury.
objective_facts: City permit records show no structural permit was ever filed for glasshouse roof truss repairs, despite co-op meeting minutes from fifteen years ago approving emergency truss repair funds.
The welds and steel bracing on the northwest and north trusses do not match the original 1923 cast-iron specifications and show non-professional fabrication marks.
A co-op incident report from six years ago documents a partial glazing and truss collapse during a spring gathering that injured Yoon Hee-ja, requiring hospitalization.
Aiko Tanaka has purchased replacement bracing hardware for the same northwest truss section three times in the past six years, each time without an accompanying work order or board approval.

case.label: The Founder's Rest

character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: As Halloran's process grinds toward more formal document requests, Mei-Lin grows newly anxious and deflects Liwei's questions about older archives by insisting they're 'not organized,' never lying outright, just controlling access; she tells no one why the southeast pier matters to her.
character_knowledge.LIWEI_CASTELLAN: Liwei has no idea what he's brushing against; he reads his mother's cagey behavior as sentimental overprotectiveness about the building's history, grows mildly frustrated, and pushes for digitizing the archive to speed things up — unaware that's the very thing that could expose her.

case.solution: In October 1923, during the rushed construction of the rooftop glasshouse, laborer Kwok-Wing Lai fell into the wet concrete pour of the southeast corner support pier and died. Crew foreman Otto Brandt, bribed by the building's original owner to avoid costly delays, labor unrest, and immigration scrutiny of undocumented Chinese laborers, had the body sealed inside the pier rather than reported, and falsified the payroll log to show Lai as simply 'discharged.' Decades later, Lai's sister — Mei-Lin Castellan's grandmother — learned the truth from a dying former crew member's letter and spent the rest of her life as the greenhouse's unofficial guardian, quietly steering every renovation and survey away from that corner. Mei-Lin inherited both the guardianship and the secret, and has never told anyone, including her own son Liwei.
objective_facts: The greenhouse's southeast corner support pier was poured in a single rushed night in October 1923, unlike the other three piers, which the original builder's ledger shows took a full week each.

Crew foreman Otto Brandt's 1923 payroll records list laborer Kwok-Wing Lai as 'discharged' the same week the southeast pier was completed, with no further employment or immigration record of him found afterward.
A 1961 letter from a former crew member to Mei-Lin's grandmother, hidden inside a false-bottomed seed tin in the greenhouse potting shed, describes what happened to Kwok-Wing Lai.
Ground-penetrating radar or structural coring at the southeast pier, as required by the developer's due-diligence survey, would almost certainly detect human remains.

case.label: The Missing Dues

character_knowledge.DECLAN_FARRAY: Rattled that Iggy has flagged one of his fabricated Vance Municipal invoices, Declan invents a more elaborate story about a 'verbal agreement' with the now-dissolved contractor, privately aware the excuse is thin and won't survive real scrutiny.
character_knowledge.IGGY_CHEN: Iggy doesn't suspect fraud; he reads the anomaly as sloppy record-keeping typical of an old co-op, logs it as a minor compliance flag, and plans to request the contractor's licensing paperwork next — with no idea he's about to unravel a crime.
character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: Mei-Lin notices Declan seems more anxious than usual about the books but assumes it's just the stress of the sale, with no suspicion of the true scale of what he's hiding.

case.solution: Over the six years since becoming treasurer, Declan Farray has diverted roughly $3,000-4,000 a month from the co-op's maintenance reserve fund — more than $240,000 in total — to cover his adult son Bryan's gambling debts and, more recently, threats from the people Bryan owes money to. Declan has hidden the shortfall behind fictitious repair invoices from a contractor that no longer exists, and has aggressively lobbied the board to delay any professional facilities audit, knowing a real estate sale would trigger exactly the kind of scrutiny that would expose the doctored books within days.
objective_facts: The co-op's reserve fund shows $241,500 less than the total of all dues collected and interest accrued since Declan Farray became treasurer six years ago.

Three invoices in the co-op's files — for a boiler replacement, roof membrane repair, and elevator motor service — list a contractor, Vance Municipal Services, that dissolved as a business four years ago and never performed the listed work.

Declan Farray's son Bryan has an active collections judgment against him, and Declan's personal bank statements show a documented pattern of cash withdrawals matching dates shortly after co-op dues deposits clear.
Any third-party financial audit tied to the developer's acquisition due diligence would cross-reference invoices against contractor licensing records within its first week.

case.label: The Borrowed Name

character_knowledge.SOO_JIN_PARK: The identity-verification letter throws Soo-Jin into private panic — she has nothing that would survive real scrutiny as 'Yoon Hee-ja' — and she downplays her fear to Aiko as general worry about paperwork while she quietly weighs stalling, forging, or fleeing.
character_knowledge.AIKO_TANAKA: Aiko notices Soo-Jin is more rattled than the letter alone explains but chalks it up to the general anxiety many newer residents feel around immigration paperwork, offering reassurance without understanding what's actually being asked of her.
character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: As board chair, Mei-Lin forwarded the identity-verification requirement to every shareholder without registering anything unusual about Yoon Hee-ja's file specifically — to her it's routine.
character_knowledge.IGGY_CHEN: Iggy is pushing the ID verification as standard due-diligence procedure across all shareholders and has no particular suspicion yet about Unit 4G.

case.solution: The rightful shareholder of Unit 4G, Yoon Hee-ja, died five years ago. Her grandniece, Soo-Jin Park, had moved in two years earlier on a caregiver visa to look after her, and — terrified of losing both her home and her immigration standing with no other family in the country — never reported the death. She has been forging Yoon Hee-ja's signature on co-op ballots, maintenance requests, and an expired lease renewal ever since, and pays dues from an account still nominally in her aunt's name. A shareholder-identity verification, which Halloran's legal team requires before any sale can close, would immediately expose that 'Yoon Hee-ja' has not appeared in person, aged, or renewed identification in five years.
objective_facts: Yoon Hee-ja's death certificate, filed with the city five years ago, has never been submitted to the co-op board or recorded in its shareholder registry.
Signatures on the last five years of annual meeting ballots attributed to Yoon Hee-ja show handwriting inconsistent with her notarized 1998 share certificate signature.
Yoon Hee-ja's driver's license and passport both expired within eight months of her death and were never renewed.
Halloran's acquisition contract requires notarized in-person identity verification of every shareholder of record before closing.

Crew foreman Otto Brandt, bribed by the building's original owner to avoid costly delays, labor unrest, and immigration scrutiny of undocumented Chinese laborers, had the body sealed inside the pier rather than reported, and falsified the payroll log to show Lai as simply 'discharged.' Decades later, Lai's sister — Mei-Lin Castellan's grandmother — learned the truth from a dying former crew member's letter and spent the rest of her life as the greenhouse's unofficial guardian, quietly steering every renovation and survey away from that corner. Mei-Lin inherited both the guardianship and the secret, and has never told anyone, including her own son Liwei.
objective_facts: The greenhouse's southeast corner support pier was poured in a single rushed night in October 1923, unlike the other three piers, which the original builder's ledger shows took a full week each.

Crew foreman Otto Brandt's 1923 payroll records list laborer Kwok-Wing Lai as 'discharged' the same week the southeast pier was completed, with no further employment or immigration record of him found afterward.
A 1961 letter from a former crew member to Mei-Lin's grandmother, hidden inside a false-bottomed seed tin in the greenhouse potting shed, describes what happened to Kwok-Wing Lai.
Ground-penetrating radar or structural coring at the southeast pier, as required by the developer's due-diligence survey, would almost certainly detect human remains.

case.label: The Missing Dues

character_knowledge.IGGY_CHEN: Has cross-checked one invoice against public contractor licensing and found no match, and privately suspects financial irregularities, though he still reads it as small-scale co-op mismanagement rather than deliberate theft.
character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: Senses Declan's nervousness at board meetings and privately worries the books won't survive real scrutiny, but chooses not to press him — she has her own reasons not to invite anyone auditing anything too closely.

case.solution: Over the six years since becoming treasurer, Declan Farray has diverted roughly $3,000-4,000 a month from the co-op's maintenance reserve fund — more than $240,000 in total — to cover his adult son Bryan's gambling debts and, more recently, threats from the people Bryan owes money to. Declan has hidden the shortfall behind fictitious repair invoices from a contractor that no longer exists, and has aggressively lobbied the board to delay any professional facilities audit, knowing a real estate sale would trigger exactly the kind of scrutiny that would expose the doctored books within days.
objective_facts: The co-op's reserve fund shows $241,500 less than the total of all dues collected and interest accrued since Declan Farray became treasurer six years ago.

Three invoices in the co-op's files — for a boiler replacement, roof membrane repair, and elevator motor service — list a contractor, Vance Municipal Services, that dissolved as a business four years ago and never performed the listed work.

Declan Farray's son Bryan has an active collections judgment against him, and Declan's personal bank statements show a documented pattern of cash withdrawals matching dates shortly after co-op dues deposits clear.
Any third-party financial audit tied to the developer's acquisition due diligence would cross-reference invoices against contractor licensing records within its first week.

case.label: The Borrowed Name

Signatures on the last five years of annual meeting ballots attributed to Yoon Hee-ja show handwriting inconsistent with her notarized 1998 share certificate signature.
Yoon Hee-ja's driver's license and passport both expired within eight months of her death and were never renewed.
Halloran's acquisition contract requires notarized in-person identity verification of every shareholder of record before closing.

case.label: The Quiet Fix

character_knowledge.DECLAN_FARRAY: Has noticed Aiko buying unusual quantities of steel bracing again and jokes about it at a board meeting, with no idea it's connected to anything more than routine maintenance.

case.solution: Fifteen years ago, a summer storm cracked two of the glasshouse's original cast-iron roof trusses. Superintendent Aiko Tanaka, under pressure from a board that couldn't afford a permitted structural repair, welded and braced the trusses herself using mismatched salvaged steel and never filed for the required city permit or follow-up inspection. The patch held for years but weakened over time; six years ago a section of glazing and truss brace gave way during a co-op gathering, badly injuring Yoon Hee-ja and contributing to the decline in her health that led to her death the following year. Aiko has quietly monitored and re-shored the same corner ever since, terrified an inspection will expose both her original unpermitted repair and her role in Yoon Hee-ja's injury.
objective_facts: City permit records show no structural permit was ever filed for glasshouse roof truss repairs, despite co-op meeting minutes from fifteen years ago approving emergency truss repair funds.
The welds and steel bracing on the northwest and north trusses do not match the original 1923 cast-iron specifications and show non-professional fabrication marks.
A co-op incident report from six years ago documents a partial glazing and truss collapse during a spring gathering that injured Yoon Hee-ja, requiring hospitalization.
Aiko Tanaka has purchased replacement bracing hardware for the same northwest truss section three times in the past six years, each time without an accompanying work order or board approval.

episodes.theme: The board convenes an emergency session as Soo-Jin's status becomes an open secret among the residents, Declan's financial cover story collapses under Iggy's audit, and Mei-Lin, desperate to postpone the coring test one more time, makes a choice that puts her own deepest secret at real risk of discovery.
episodes.intro_note: By morning, what happened in 4G is no longer just between Liwei and Soo-Jin — someone talked, or guessed, and the building is humming with half-versions of the truth. With Declan's invoices already fraying and the coring test only days away, the co-op has more secrets straining at once than it has ever had to hold.
episodes.outro_note: The false hazard claim buys a few days of delay — but it also draws a city safety inspector who wants to examine the southeast corner in person, on Mei-Lin's own terms this time, not Halloran's. For the first time in her life, the woman who has spent five decades keeping people away from that pier has invited someone closer to it than she ever meant to.

Declan Farray's son Bryan has an active collections judgment against him, and Declan's personal bank statements show a documented pattern of cash withdrawals matching dates shortly after co-op dues deposits clear.
Any third-party financial audit tied to the developer's acquisition due diligence would cross-reference invoices against contractor licensing records within its first week.

case.label: The Borrowed Name

character_knowledge.SOO_JIN_PARK: She finally says it plainly to the people gathered in her aunt's front room — that Yoon Hee-ja died five years ago, that she forged the signatures, that she was afraid of losing the only home and legal foothold she had. She doesn't ask for forgiveness so much as brace for whatever comes next.
character_knowledge.LIWEI_CASTELLAN: He's known the truth since the file room, and today he makes his choice plain: he argues for shielding her from Halloran's identity verification rather than exposing her to it, and asks her, quietly, to let him actually help this time instead of just knowing.
character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: She proposes the board quietly reclassify the shareholding through a small internal transfer rather than trigger Halloran's outside notarization requirement — a fix she can offer with real conviction, since burying an inconvenient truth to protect this building is a skill she has had a great deal of practice with.
character_knowledge.AIKO_TANAKA: Having loved Yoon Hee-ja like family, she is the first to say Soo-Jin should stay, and the loudest — her grief for the aunt folding easily into fierce, protective loyalty toward the niece who never stopped tending her.

case.solution: The rightful shareholder of Unit 4G, Yoon Hee-ja, died five years ago. Her grandniece, Soo-Jin Park, had moved in two years earlier on a caregiver visa to look after her, and — terrified of losing both her home and her immigration standing with no other family in the country — never reported the death. She has been forging Yoon Hee-ja's signature on co-op ballots, maintenance requests, and an expired lease renewal ever since, and pays dues from an account still nominally in her aunt's name. A shareholder-identity verification, which Halloran's legal team requires before any sale can close, would immediately expose that 'Yoon Hee-ja' has not appeared in person, aged, or renewed identification in five years.
objective_facts: Yoon Hee-ja's death certificate, filed with the city five years ago, has never been submitted to the co-op board or recorded in its shareholder registry.
Signatures on the last five years of annual meeting ballots attributed to Yoon Hee-ja show handwriting inconsistent with her notarized 1998 share certificate signature.
Yoon Hee-ja's driver's license and passport both expired within eight months of her death and were never renewed.
Halloran's acquisition contract requires notarized in-person identity verification of every shareholder of record before closing.

case.label: The Quiet Fix

character_knowledge.AIKO_TANAKA: She stands in her own workshop and tells the board everything — the unpermitted welds fifteen years ago, the collapse six years ago, the guilt she has carried for what happened to Yoon Hee-ja ever since — and accepts whatever judgment follows, including the possibility of losing the only job she's held in this country for thirty years.
character_knowledge.DECLAN_FARRAY: As treasurer, damaged as his own standing now is, he's the one who calculates what a permitted retrofit will actually cost and argues the co-op owes Aiko the chance to help fix what she broke rather than simply firing the person who has kept the building running for three decades.
character_knowledge.MEI_LIN_CASTELLAN: She votes, as chair, for Aiko to keep her position under a supervised, fully permitted repair plan — genuine mercy for a woman she's trusted for years, though she's also acutely aware that a superintendent who owes the board her job is a superintendent who won't ask twice why one corner still isn't on the repair schedule.
character_knowledge.SOO_JIN_PARK: Hearing at last the real reason behind her great-aunt's fall, she's furious and grieving in the same breath — but when the vote comes to keep Aiko on, she doesn't oppose it, telling Aiko only that Yoon Hee-ja was never someone who believed in throwing people away.

case.solution: Fifteen years ago, a summer storm cracked two of the glasshouse's original cast-iron roof trusses. Superintendent Aiko Tanaka, under pressure from a board that couldn't afford a permitted structural repair, welded and braced the trusses herself using mismatched salvaged steel and never filed for the required city permit or follow-up inspection. The patch held for years but weakened over time; six years ago a section of glazing and truss brace gave way during a co-op gathering, badly injuring Yoon Hee-ja and contributing to the decline in her health that led to her death the following year. Aiko has quietly monitored and re-shored the same corner ever since, terrified an inspection will expose both her original unpermitted repair and her role in Yoon Hee-ja's injury.
objective_facts: City permit records show no structural permit was ever filed for glasshouse roof truss repairs, despite co-op meeting minutes from fifteen years ago approving emergency truss repair funds.
The welds and steel bracing on the northwest and north trusses do not match the original 1923 cast-iron specifications and show non-professional fabrication marks.
A co-op incident report from six years ago documents a partial glazing and truss collapse during a spring gathering that injured Yoon Hee-ja, requiring hospitalization.
Aiko Tanaka has purchased replacement bracing hardware for the same northwest truss section three times in the past six years, each time without an accompanying work order or board approval.
